name: Lawyer
summary: A licensed legal professional who advises clients and advocates in disputes—evolving from Roman orators and medieval pleaders into modern bar-admitted attorneys.
duties: Research precedent, draft contracts and briefs, represent clients in negotiations and court, and maintain ethical compliance.
skills: Legal reasoning, writing, oral advocacy, client counseling, case management.
tools: Law libraries, Westlaw-style databases, pleading templates, court e-filing systems.
regions: Global legal professions; strong traditions in England, France, and US state bars.
originLabel: Advocates in Athenian and Roman forums
educationPath: Law degrees, bar exams, continuing legal education, clerkships.
modernSectors: Law firms, in-house counsel, legal aid, government, solo practice.

events.label: Professional bar associations
events.description: Self-regulating bars standardize admission and ethics in many jurisdictions.

name: Judge
summary: An official who presides over courts, interprets law, and renders decisions—central to governance from tribal elders to constitutional benches.
duties: Hear cases, rule on motions, instruct juries, write opinions, and manage courtroom procedure.
skills: Impartiality, legal mastery, temperament, administrative leadership, clear writing.
tools: Bench, gavel, court reporters, case management systems, law clerks.
regions: Every state with courts; supreme courts in national capitals.
originLabel: Temple and palace adjudication in early law codes
educationPath: Prior legal career, judicial appointment or election, ongoing ethics training.
modernSectors: Trial courts, appellate benches, administrative tribunals, international courts.

events.label: Magna Carta
events.description: Charter traditions influence independent judiciary concepts in English law.

name: Barrister
summary: A courtroom advocate in British-derived systems who speaks before higher courts—separated from solicitors who handle client intake and paperwork.
duties: Plead in court, examine witnesses, write opinions, and take silk for senior leadership of the bar.
skills: Oral argument, quick thinking, legal research, wig-and-gown courtroom decorum.
tools: Briefs, law reports, chambers libraries, court lists.
regions: England and Wales, Ireland, Australia, Hong Kong, and other common-law jurisdictions.
originLabel: Inns of Court advocacy training in London
educationPath: Law degree, bar professional training, pupillage in chambers.
modernSectors: Criminal and commercial chambers, appellate practice, public inquiries.

events.label: Inns of Court maturity
events.description: Four Inns consolidate advocacy training for English barristers.

name: Solicitor
summary: A lawyer who manages client matters, drafts documents, and instructs barristers in split common-law professions—analogous to general practice attorneys elsewhere.
duties: Advise clients, handle conveyancing and contracts, prepare cases for trial counsel, and manage firm operations.
skills: Client service, transactional drafting, negotiation, matter budgeting, referral networks.
tools: Precedent banks, due diligence checklists, CRM, e-conveyancing portals.
regions: UK, Ireland, Australia; contrasts with unified bar in US.
originLabel: Letters and petitions for manor and royal courts
educationPath: Law degree, legal practice course, training contracts at firms.
modernSectors: City firms, high street practices, government legal service, in-house.

events.label: Judicature Acts
events.description: English court reforms clarify solicitor versus barrister roles in unified court structure.

name: Prosecutor
summary: A government lawyer who brings criminal charges on behalf of the state—evolving from crown prosecutors and district attorneys into specialized advocacy offices.
duties: Screen police cases, file charges, negotiate pleas, try cases, and protect victims' procedural rights.
skills: Trial advocacy, ethics, investigation review, public accountability, resilience.
tools: Evidence databases, grand jury procedures (US), charging standards manuals.
regions: District attorneys in US counties, Crown Prosecution Service in UK, parquet in France.
originLabel: State prosecution in expanding royal courts
educationPath: Law degree, bar admission, prosecutor training academies.
modernSectors: County DA offices, DOJ divisions, international tribunals, anticorruption units.

events.label: Victim-centered reforms
events.description: Many systems expand prosecutor victim liaison and domestic violence specialization.

name: Public Defender
summary: A defense lawyer appointed to represent people who cannot afford counsel—institutionalized as a constitutional right in many democracies.
duties: Interview clients in custody, investigate cases, negotiate pleas, and try matters with heavy caseloads.
skills: Client rapport under stress, speedy triage, trial skills, empathy, systemic advocacy.
tools: Investigator referrals, sentencing guidelines, misdemeanor and felony calendars.
regions: US county defender offices, legal aid societies in UK and Europe.
educationPath: Law degree, criminal defense clinics, public defender training programs.
modernSectors: Public defender offices, legal aid nonprofits, appellate defender units.

events.label: Gideon v. Wainwright
events.description: US Supreme Court requires states to provide counsel for indigent felony defendants.

name: Notary
summary: An official who witnesses signatures, administers oaths, and certifies documents—descended from Roman notarii and still vital in civil-law property transfers.
duties: Verify identity, witness signings, keep registers, authenticate copies, and refuse improper acts.
skills: Attention to detail, fraud detection, procedural knowledge, impartiality.
tools: Seal stamps, journals, ID verification systems, document archives.
regions: Latin Europe, Louisiana, Latin America; less central in ordinary US practice.

events.label: Codified civil law
events.description: Napoleonic codes embed notaries in property and inheritance workflows across Europe.

name: Bailiff
summary: A court officer who maintains order, serves papers, and manages juries—also used for debt enforcement and estate officers in historical English usage.
duties: Secure courtrooms, escort defendants, execute writs, and assist judges with jury logistics.
skills: Authority, de-escalation, procedure knowledge, physical presence, paperwork accuracy.
tools: Badges, service forms, radios, restraints when authorized.
regions: Common-law court systems; high bailiff titles in Channel Islands and UK ceremonial roles.
originLabel: Sheriff's and manor court officers in England

events.label: Professional court security
events.description: Modern court buildings employ dedicated bailiff and security staffing models.

name: Sheriff
summary: A county law enforcement chief elected or appointed in many US jurisdictions—title surviving from Anglo-Saxon shire reeves who combined tax, court, and policing powers.
duties: Operate jails, serve civil process, provide patrol in unincorporated areas, and coordinate with courts.
skills: Law enforcement leadership, budgeting, politics, emergency response coordination.
tools: Patrol fleets, jail facilities, warrants databases, body cameras.
regions: United States counties especially; historical England and Commonwealth parallels.

events.label: Frontier county policing
events.description: US westward expansion embeds elected sheriffs in county governance mythology.

name: Magistrate
summary: A judicial officer handling minor crimes, preliminary hearings, or civil small claims—below trial judges on many court ladders.
duties: Set bail, approve warrants, hear misdemeanors, mediate minor disputes, and route serious cases upward.
skills: Procedural efficiency, fairness, community awareness, stamina for high volume.
tools: Bench manuals, scheduling systems, night court dockets.
regions: England magistrates' benches, US lower courts, civil-law investigating magistrates historically.

events.label: Professional magistracy
events.description: Many jurisdictions train paid magistrates alongside lay benches.

name: Diplomat
summary: A state representative who negotiates treaties, protects citizens abroad, and channels political communication between governments.
duties: Report on foreign politics, negotiate agreements, host receptions, crisis-manage evacuations, and brief home capitals.
skills: Languages, tact, writing cables, cultural fluency, discretion, endurance of posting hardships.
tools: Embassy secure communications, diplomatic passports, negotiation texts, protocol guides.
regions: Foreign ministries worldwide; Vienna Convention framework for missions.
educationPath: Foreign service exams, political science, area studies, language training.
modernSectors: Embassies, consulates, UN missions, EU delegations, special envoys.

events.label: Congress of Vienna
events.description: Modern diplomatic corps professionalize after Napoleonic wars.

name: Ambassador
summary: The senior diplomatic representative of one state to another—bearing plenipotentiary authority and embodying ceremonial head-of-mission duties.
duties: Lead embassies, advise host governments, present credentials, manage country teams, and represent head-of-state functions.
skills: Leadership, high-level negotiation, political judgment, public speaking, crisis diplomacy.
tools: Embassy staff, residence events, secure comms, policy clearance chains from capital.
regions: Capital embassies globally; dean of diplomatic corps traditions in major cities.
originLabel: Resident ambassadors in Italian Renaissance statecraft

events.label: Vienna Convention
events.description: Codifies ambassador immunities and embassy functions in international law.

name: Civil Servant
summary: A permanent government employee who administers policy without changing with every election—professionalized to reduce patronage in 19th-century reforms.
duties: Implement regulations, process applications, brief elected officials, and manage public programs.
skills: Procedure mastery, neutrality, writing, project management, stakeholder navigation.
tools: Case management systems, policy memos, intranet knowledge bases, FOIA workflows.
regions: Westminster systems, EU commissions, US federal and state bureaucracies.
educationPath: Public policy degrees, civil service exams, rotational graduate programs.
modernSectors: Ministries, agencies, regulators, municipal administration, international orgs.

events.label: US Pendleton Act
events.description: Merit hiring reduces spoils appointments in US federal jobs.

name: Mayor
summary: An elected or appointed head of a city government who sets local agendas and oversees municipal departments—from medieval commune leaders to modern metro executives.
duties: Propose budgets, appoint managers, represent cities publicly, coordinate emergency response, and chair councils.
skills: Politics, coalition building, media, fiscal oversight, community visibility.
tools: City hall staff, council agendas, emergency operations centers, ribbon-cutting calendars.
regions: Cities worldwide; strong mayor models in US and weak mayor/council-manager variants.

events.label: Progressive municipal reform
events.description: US reformers professionalize city departments under elected mayors or managers.

name: Legislator
summary: A member of a lawmaking body who drafts, debates, and votes on statutes—embodying representative government in parliaments and congresses.
duties: Sponsor bills, committee work, constituency service, oversight hearings, and coalition negotiation.
skills: Public speaking, policy analysis, fundraising (where applicable), negotiation, stamina.
tools: Bill drafting offices, committee rooms, voting boards, constituency offices.
regions: Congresses, parliaments, state assemblies, city councils with lawmaking power.
educationPath: Varied backgrounds; law and policy experience common; election campaigns.
modernSectors: National legislatures, provincial assemblies, supranational parliaments.

events.label: Modern constitutional legislatures
events.description: US Congress and French revolutionary assemblies model elected lawmaking at scale.

name: Lobbyist
summary: A professional advocate who persuades legislators and regulators on behalf of clients—named for waiting in legislative lobbies to influence votes.
duties: Track bills, draft amendments, arrange meetings, build coalitions, and file disclosure reports.
skills: Networking, policy detail, persuasion, ethics navigation, campaign finance literacy.
tools: Bill trackers, disclosure filings, PAC contributions where legal, stakeholder maps.
regions: Washington DC, Brussels, state capitals; regulated differently by jurisdiction.
originLabel: Railroad and tariff lobbying in US capitals
educationPath: Political staff alumni networks, law degrees, issue advocacy apprenticeships.
modernSectors: Trade associations, corporate government affairs, NGOs, consulting firms.

events.label: US lobbying disclosure
events.description: Federal registration requirements begin formalizing lobbyist transparency.

name: Police Officer
summary: A uniformed peace officer who enforces law, responds to emergencies, and investigates crimes—institutionalized as modern municipal police in the 19th century.
duties: Patrol beats, answer calls, write reports, arrest suspects, testify in court, and engage communities.
skills: De-escalation, report writing, physical fitness, legal knowledge, judgment under stress.
tools: Radio, body camera, firearm where authorized, cruiser, databases.
regions: City police globally; Peel's London model influential worldwide.
educationPath: Police academies, field training officers, continuing use-of-force training.
modernSectors: Municipal PDs, county sheriffs, transit police, campus police.

events.label: Peelian principles
events.description: Robert Peel's London force professionalizes preventive policing doctrine.

name: Detective
summary: An investigator who specializes in gathering evidence for serious crimes—plainclothes counterparts to uniform patrol officers.
duties: Interview witnesses, analyze evidence, serve warrants, build cases with prosecutors, and testify.
skills: Interview technique, pattern recognition, report writing, patience, ethical evidence handling.
tools: Forensic kits, case files, surveillance gear, lab submissions, interview rooms.
regions: Police departments worldwide; Scotland Yard traditions influential in fiction and practice.
educationPath: Promotion from patrol, detective training schools, specialized fraud or homicide units.
modernSectors: Homicide, fraud, cybercrime, narcotics, cold case units.

events.label: Forensic science
events.description: Fingerprints and labs augment detective work in urban departments.

name: Constable
summary: A parish or hundred peace officer in pre-modern England who apprehended offenders and executed warrants—ancestor to modern policing titles still used in Commonwealth forces.
duties: Raise hue and cry, transport prisoners, serve magistrate orders, and collect local rates in some eras.
skills: Local knowledge, physical courage, rudimentary law literacy, community authority.
tools: Staff, handcuffs historically, warrant papers, parish stocks.
regions: Rural England and colonies through the 19th century; title persists in some modern police ranks.

events.label: Professional police replace watches
events.description: Municipal forces supersede unpaid constable watches in many English cities.

name: Prison Guard
summary: A corrections officer who supervises incarcerated people—managing safety and routines in jails and prisons since purpose-built penitentiaries.
duties: Head counts, escort movement, break up conflicts, inspect cells, and document incidents.
skills: De-escalation, vigilance, rules enforcement, emotional regulation, teamwork.
tools: Keys, radios, restraints, surveillance monitors, body armor in high-security units.
regions: National prison systems; Auburn and Pennsylvania models influenced global design.
educationPath: Corrections academies, on-the-job training, crisis intervention certification.
modernSectors: State prisons, county jails, federal BOP, private corrections contractors.

events.label: Prisoners' rights era
events.description: Courts and riots push procedural reforms and officer training standards.

name: Tax Collector
summary: A revenue official who assesses and gathers taxes for states—feared in history as Roman publicani and still central to modern revenue agencies.
duties: Audit returns, issue assessments, enforce payment, educate taxpayers, and pursue delinquency cases.
skills: Accounting literacy, negotiation, firmness, regulatory knowledge, customer service in voluntary compliance systems.
tools: Assessment software, liens, audit checklists, call center systems.
regions: National revenue services: IRS, HMRC, CRA, and municipal collectors.
originLabel: Temple and royal tithe collectors in early states
educationPath: Accounting degrees, revenue service training, enforcement certifications.
modernSectors: Income tax authorities, customs, property tax offices, VAT administrations.

events.label: US income tax permanence
events.description: 16th Amendment era expands federal income tax administration employment.

name: Coroner
summary: An official who investigates unexplained deaths—medieval crown officers evolved into medical examiner systems in many jurisdictions.
duties: Scene investigations, order autopsies, hold inquests, determine manner of death, and testify.
skills: Forensic awareness, impartiality, family sensitivity, legal procedure, report writing.
tools: Investigation kits, death certificates, toxicology referrals, inquest records.
regions: England coroner tradition; US mix of elected coroners and appointed medical examiners.
educationPath: Medical degree for MEs; law enforcement or medical backgrounds for coroners depending on jurisdiction.
modernSectors: County coroner offices, regional medical examiner departments, mass disaster teams.

events.label: Medical examiner adoption
events.description: US cities shift from lay coroners to physician-run death investigation.

name: Registrar
summary: A civil records officer who registers births, deaths, marriages, and land titles—backbone of documentary statehood since parish registers.
duties: Issue certificates, maintain indexes, officiate civil marriages where authorized, and prevent fraud.
skills: Record keeping, customer service, legal requirements mastery, data accuracy.
tools: Registration databases, vault archives, seal stamps, appointment scheduling.
regions: Civil registration districts in UK; county recorders in US states.
originLabel: Parish registers mandated in Tudor England

events.label: UK civil registration
events.description: General Register Office centralizes vital records beyond parish church books.

name: City Manager
summary: A professional administrator hired to run municipal operations under elected councils—the council-manager form spreading in 20th-century US reform governments.
duties: Oversee departments, prepare budgets, hire/fire staff, implement council policy, and manage infrastructure.
skills: Public administration, finance, labor relations, political neutrality, long-range planning.
tools: Capital improvement plans, performance dashboards, union contracts, council packets.
regions: US council-manager cities; parallels in city chief executives globally.
educationPath: MPA degrees, assistant manager career ladders, ICMA credentialing.
modernSectors: Mid-size US cities, counties, special districts, municipal consulting.

events.label: ICMA founded
events.description: International City Managers Association professionalizes the occupation.
